coredata.dc:title: Multi-mode fluorescence sensing detection based on one core-shell structure quantum dots via different types of mechanisms
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Wei
Fan, Jun
Xia, Yan
Yin, Xuebo
coredata.dc:description: Multi-target sensing completed with single chemosensor is cost-effective and pretty attractive for the analysis of multiple analytes in single sample. CdTe/CdS QDs show high fluorescence quantum yield as a probe for the sensing purpose, but only single target is often detected. Here we proposed that three kinds of different response mechanisms were realized using CdTe/CdS QDs with the simultaneous sensing of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2−, and IO3 − as model. The specific binding affinity was used to detect Fe3+ with the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism. CdTe/CdS QDs showed intensive fluorescent response to Cr2O7 2− owing to the inner filter effect (IFE). IO3 − is an oxidant and reacts to surface functional groups of CdTe/CdS QDs with oxidation quenching (OQ) mechanism for the selective detection of IO3 −. The three targets recorded with the three mechanisms were obviously clustered even they were tested at different concentrations. Thus, the targets could be differentiated with each other through their different responses of PET, IFE, and OQ. The linear ranges for the selective detection of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2−, and IO3 − were 5.0–100.0, 20.0–140.0 and 1.0–80.0 μM, with the limits of detection as low as 4.1, 9.7, and 0.9 μM, respectively. The proposed methods were satisfactorily applied to accurately detect Fe3+, Cr2O7 2−, and IO3 − in real samples and it was further detected iodate and the total amount of iodine in table salt samples. The proposed multi-target detection strategy with the single fluorescence single from single QDs will stimulate the application of the other matrix with abundant response with enhance sensing efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CdTe/CdS QDs
Multi-mode sensing
Fluorescence detection
Fe3+
Cr2O7 2−
IO3

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent detecting experiments of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and IO3 − in aqueous solution
Detection of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and IO3 − in real samples
Design, preparation, and characterization of CdTe/CdS QDs
Discrimination Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and IO3 − integrated into CdTe/CdS QDs
Multi-mode sensing Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and IO3 − integrated into CdTe/CdS QDs
Detection of Fe3+ in a PET manner
Detection of Cr2O7 2− in an IFE manner
Detection of IO3 − in an OQ manner
Detection of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and IO3 − in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21874073 ) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CdTe/CdS QDs (inset: SAED image of CdTe/CdS quantum dots). (B) XRD pattern of the CdTe QDs and CdTe/CdS QDs. (C) FT-IR spectrum of the MPA and CdTe/CdS QDs. (D) EDX analysis of CdTe/CdS QDs. (E) Elemental mapping images of Te, S, and Cd for CdTe/CdS QDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra of the CdTe/CdS QDs (excitation wavelengths ranging from 270 to 320 nm). (B) The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the CdTe/CdS QDs. (C) The effect of ionic strength on the FL intensity of the CdTe/CdS QDs.

(A) FL spectra of the CdTe/CdS QDs responding to Fe3+ with various concentrations of Fe3+. The inset is the linear relation of (F0-F)/F0 and the concentrations of Fe3+. (B) FL spectra of the CdTe/CdS QDs responding to Cr2O7 2− with various concentrations of Cr2O7 2−. The inset is the linear relation of (F0-F)/F0 and the concentrations of Cr2O7 2−. (C) FL spectra of the CdTe/CdS QDs responding to IO3 − with various concentrations of IO3 −. The inset is the linear relation of (F0-F)/F0 and the concentrations of IO3 −. (D) Three-Dimensional coordinate fingerprint mapping of three mechanism obtained through multidimensional colorimetric sensor array against various concentrations of target ions from 10 to 70 μM. (the X, Y, and Z axes represent the FL intensities of the IFE, OQ, and PET.)

(A) Selective FL response of the CdTe/CdS QDs in the presence of 200 μM various interfering ions. (B) The FL spectra of the CdTe/CdS QDs, QDs on addition of Fe3+ and QDs on addition of Fe3+ and EDTA. Inset: the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the Fe3+ and the QDs in the presence of Fe3+.

(A) Selective FL response of the CdTe/CdS QDs in the presence of 200 μM various interfering ions. (B) The UV–vis absorption spectra of QDs, Cr(VI) and the QDs in presence of Cr(VI) ions. (C) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr2O7 2−, excitation spectra of the CdTe/CdS QDs and emission spectra.

(A) Selective FL response of the CdTe/CdS QDs in the presence of 100 μM different anions ions in BR buffer solution. (B) Images of the mixture solution at different concentration of IO3 − under UV light. (C) Photographs with the color change of the mixture after adding the starch to the photo and adding phosphoric acid. Bottle A: The QDs solution in presence of iodate in the BR buffer solution, bottle B: The solution of MPA containing iodate in the BR buffer solution.
Triple sensing modes for Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and IO3 − are achieved by one CdTe/CdS QDs-based platform via different types of mechanisms including PET, IFE and OQ.
Results for the detection of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and IO3 − in real samples.
Determination of iodate and the total amount of iodine in table salt.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The given iodate value was 30–50 mg/kg.

head.ce:title: Multi-mode fluorescence sensing detection based on one core-shell structure quantum dots via different types of mechanisms

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Science, State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology and Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition, College of Chemistry, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology and Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Analytical Science, State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology and Tianjin Key Laboratory of Biosensing and Molecular Recognition, College of Chemistry, Nankai University, Tianjin, 300071, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multi-target sensing completed with single chemosensor is cost-effective and pretty attractive for the analysis of multiple analytes in single sample. CdTe/CdS QDs show high fluorescence quantum yield as a probe for the sensing purpose, but only single target is often detected. Here we proposed that three kinds of different response mechanisms were realized using CdTe/CdS QDs with the simultaneous sensing of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2−, and IO3 − as model. The specific binding affinity was used to detect Fe3+ with the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism. CdTe/CdS QDs showed intensive fluorescent response to Cr2O7 2− owing to the inner filter effect (IFE). IO3 − is an oxidant and reacts to surface functional groups of CdTe/CdS QDs with oxidation quenching (OQ) mechanism for the selective detection of IO3 −. The three targets recorded with the three mechanisms were obviously clustered even they were tested at different concentrations. Thus, the targets could be differentiated with each other through their different responses of PET, IFE, and OQ. The linear ranges for the selective detection of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2−, and IO3 − were 5.0–100.0, 20.0–140.0 and 1.0–80.0 μM, with the limits of detection as low as 4.1, 9.7, and 0.9 μM, respectively. The proposed methods were satisfactorily applied to accurately detect Fe3+, Cr2O7 2−, and IO3 − in real samples and it was further detected iodate and the total amount of iodine in table salt samples. The proposed multi-target detection strategy with the single fluorescence single from single QDs will stimulate the application of the other matrix with abundant response with enhance sensing efficiency.

Discrimination and sensitivity detection of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and IO3 − based on the prepared high-quality CdTe/CdS quantum dots via PET, IFE and OQ mechanisms.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Multi-target sensing devices are receiving increasing interest for analysts because it is implemented in a single sensor rather than multiple probes, thereby providing higher accuracy and/or diversity [1–5]. Multi-target sensing strategies are often carried out by incorporating multiple signaling units in single molecule as lab on-a-molecule, such as electrochemiluminescence, fluorescence, and absorbance, but the molecules required complex design and preparation [6]. Various multi-target detections were realized thanks for the boom of nanoparticles. Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots (QDs) were used to distinguish proteins with their light scattering signal, short-lived fluorescence, and long-lived phosphorescence [1]. “Lab-on-MOFs” were proposed to realize multi-target detection with the fluorescence of single white-emitting MOFs by adjusting the scale of Eu3+, Tb3+ and Dy3+ ions without any post-modification or encapsulation of the dye [7]. However, the multi-signal detection required requires different apparatus [8], and accurate quantitative detection is often difficult to be performed. Despite the huge challenges, a broad-spectrum multi-target analysis system with a single chemical sensor is still urgently needed.

Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) attracted much attention due to their unique advantages over traditional fluorescent dyes [9], such as size-tunable emission [10], high fluorescent quantum yield [11,12], large stokes shift [13], and high resistance to photobleaching [14–16]. The synthesis of core-shell quantum dots not only realizes high quantum yield and long fluorescence lifetime but also decreases the biological toxicity of single-core QDs [17,18]. The core-shell CdTe/CdS nanocrystal has been studied as chemosensors, responding to analytes in various luminous mechanisms, including the inner filter effect (IFE), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), and oxidative quenching (OQ). IFE is the phenomenon that the fluorescence of QDs is weakened or quenched due to the absorption of analytes, which act as light-absorbing materials and due to one potential prerequisite is the relatively large Stokes shift of the QDs [19–22]. PET is a process of excited-state electron transfer, through which excited electrons transfer from donor to acceptor [23,24]. OQ is that the functional groups on the QD surface are destroyed by oxidants to result to fluorescence quenching. Different response mechanisms are related to different targets, so high selectivity is obtained and multi-target detection could be realized with the single signal source from the single QDs [25].

Iron is an essential element in all living things, and its deficit or overload can affect the health of the body [26–29]. Chromium (VI) is recognized as a more toxic heavy metal ion given its high carcinogenicity [30–33]. Iodine is an essential element of life, and the most effective method to deal with iodine-deficiency is to add KIO3 to table salt [34,35]. Thus, it is considerable to use reliable methods to detect iron, chromium(VI), and iodate, simultaneously. Here, we propose a strategy that single sensor with single fluorescence source to achieve quantitative detection of multiple targets with high selectivity and high sensitivity with Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and IO3 − as the targets.

To this end, highly luminescent CdTe/CdS QDs with a quantum yield of 38.5% were facilely prepared by a hydrothermal method using MPA as a stabilizer. The as-synthesized CdTe/CdS QDs were used to detect Fe3+ in HAc-NaAc buffer solution, Cr2O7 2− in PBS buffer solution, and IO3 − in Britton-Robinson buffer solution. As shown in Scheme 1 , binding affinity realized the detection of Fe3+ with PET mechanism; IFE mechanism resulted in intensive fluorescence of CdTe/CdS QDs to respond Cr2O7 2−; IO3 − oxidized the surface functional groups of CdTe/CdS QDs for the selective detection of IO3 − with oxidation quenching (OQ) mechanism. The singles of three targets recorded with the three mechanisms were obviously clustered and thus differentiate the targets with each other. CdTe/CdS QDs were successfully applied to the actual detection of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2−, and IO3 − in water samples. The CdTe/CdS QDs were also used to the determination of the total amount of iodate and iodine in table salt samples, on which few researchers reported previously. Therefore, a novel method was developed, that multiple response mechanisms from single CdTe/CdS QDs with single signal source are used to differentiate and detect multiple targets by simple switching the buffer solutions.

Zn(NO3)2, AgNO3, CaCl2, NiSO4·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, FeSO4, MgSO4, MnCl2, Pb(NO3)2, KSCN, NaHCO3, CuSO4·5H2O and Na2SO4 were obtained from Guangfu technology development Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). HAc, H3BO3, HCl and H3PO4 were obtained from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). PBS buffer solution was purchased from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). KIO3, HNO3, CH3COONa, KBrO3, KMnO4, KNO3, NaOH, NaBH4, AlCl3, CrCl3, NaCl, K2CrO7, KBr, MnCl2, KI were provided by Fengchuan Chemical Reagents (Tianjin, China). NaOH, CdCl2·2.5H2O, Thiourea (99.8%), Tellurium powder (99.9%) were purchased from Aladin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), Acetone and 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA, 99.9%) were supported by Macklin (Shanghai, China). All chemicals and reagents used were at least of analytical grade.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and EDX studies were carried out with the TecnaiG2 F20, FEI Co. (America). Most of the fluorescence experiments were carried out on a FL-4600 Fluorescence Spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). PXRD patterns from an angle range of 10° to 60° were recorded on a D/max-2500 diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) with a scanning speed of 4° min−1. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was obtained by Bruker TENSOR 27 Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, over the ranging from 400 to 4000 cm−1. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a U-3600-visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The images are taken by an SLR camera of Canon EOS 700D.

CdTe quantum dots were synthesized according to the published method with minor modifications [36]. Typically, 25.4 mg (0.2 mmol) of tellurium powder and 40 mg (1.06 mmol) of NaBH4 were dissolved with 10 mL deionized water in a 25 mL flask under nitrogen atmosphere protection. The mixture reaction was held in a 90 °C oil bath for 40 min under vigorous stirring to get a dark red clarified NaHTe solution. Then, 91.2 mg (0.4 mmol) of CdCl2·2.5H2O and 76 μL (0.8 mmol) of MPA were mixed in the reaction, and the mixture was adjusted pH value to 10.0 with 2 M NaOH solution dropwise. Under agitation, 4.0 mL of freshly prepared NaHTe solution was added to the Cd precursor reaction mixture. Then, the reaction solution was refluxed at 100 °C for 30 min under the protection of N2.

As reported in the literature procedure [11], the obtained CdTe QDs reaction solution 1.6 mmol of thiourea solution was added and adjusted pH value of 10.0 with 1 M NaOH aqueous solution. Under N2 atmosphere, the reaction mixture was heated in an oil bath to 100 °C for the growth of CdTe/CdS core-shell nanocrystals. For purpose of removing the excess Cd2+ and MPA from the product, the resulting solution was dialyzed in NaOH solution (0.001 mol L−1) by using the dialysis membrane (1000 Da MWCO) for 2 days and then stored at 4 °C in the dark for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detecting experiments of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and IO3 − in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: In the experiments, 3 mL of the assay solutions contained 200 μL CdTe/CdS QDs and various concentrations of Fe3+ in the sodium acetate-acetic acid buffer solution (0.03 mol L−1 pH 5.0), and Cr2O7 2− into PBS buffer solution (0.01 mol L−1 pH 5.0). The FL spectra were recorded under an excitation wavelength of 280 nm after being shaken for 20 min and 30 min, respectively. For the detection of IO3 −, different concentrations of IO3 − solution were added to the Britton-Robinson buffer solution (0.04 mol L−1, pH 6.0) including QDs solution (100 μL) and then the FL spectrum was recorded after shaking for 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and IO3 − in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The lake water samples were sampled from the Xinkai and Matti lakes of Nankai University (Tianjin, China). The lake water was pretreated with a filter paper and a 0.45 μm filter membrane to drive out the solid suspensions and other impurities. Different concentrations of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− were spiked to the resultant lake water samples, respectively. The tap water samples were spiked with various concentrations of IO3 − and the recovery was measured according to the recommended method. Table salt samples were obtained from local markets and dried at 100 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, trace iodide is oxidized with bromine to form iodates, and then tested by the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design, preparation, and characterization of CdTe/CdS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: We proposed that CdS were used as the core to grow CdTe/CdS QDs-based sensor directly to improve the emission and decrease the biological toxicity of the QDs. The core-shell structure of CdTe/CdS QDs was prepared in aqueous phase by reaction between Cd2+ and NaHTe solution in the presence of MPA as stabilizer. The morphology, element distribution and chemical structure of the synthesized CdTe/CdS QDs were characterized by TEM, EDX, XRD and FT-IR techniques. The TEM images in Fig. 1A displayed that the CdTe/CdS QDs were uniform black spots with a good monodispersed spherical morphology. It can be observed from the inset of Fig. 1A, the well-defined diffraction spots of the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern verified the crystalline structure of the hollow nanospheres, which agreed well with that between the cubic CdTe and CdS phase [37]. The XRD patterns of CdTe were presented in Fig. 1B, it is consistent with the characteristic cubic zinc blende CdTe pattern with diffractive peaks at 23.8°, 39.3°, and 46.6° corresponding to the (1 1 1), (2 2 0), and (3 1 1) crystallographic planes, respectively [38]. While the CdTe/CdS pattern shifts slightly to the higher angles located between the cubic CdTe and CdS phase, which in accordance with the smaller lattice constant of CdTe compared to CdS (due to the growth of CdS shell) [39]. It further confirms the formation of the CdTe/CdS core-shell structure rather than CdxTe1 − xSy alloy structure. Fourier transform-infrared (FTIR) spectra of MPA and CdTe/CdS were recorded and compared with each other (Fig. 1C). For the infrared spectrum of MPA, the broad bands at 2575, 2950 and 1712 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of O−H, S−H, and CO. Meanwhile, the disappearance of the stretching vibration peak at 2575 cm−1 (S−H) was due to the covalent bonding between S and Cd atom on the CdTe/CdS QDs surfaces. Two characteristic absorption bands centered at 1599 and 1397 cm−1 similar carbonyl absorption were attributed to CO stretching vibrations, which are the peaks unique to carboxylates [40]. Therefore, the surface of the CdTe/CdS QDs was terminated with MPA can be verified by the FTIR. Moreover, Fig. 1D shows the EDS analysis showed peaks corresponding to Cd, Te and S, confirming the elemental composition of MPA capped CdTe/CdS QDs. The elemental mapping analysis also showed that the concerning elements were homogeneously distributed on CdTe/CdS QDs (Fig. 1E).

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the synthesized CdTe/CdS were tested. As shown in Fig. 2A, the absorption spectrum indicated that the maximum exciton absorption peak of CdTe/CdS quantum dots is at 518 nm. The fluorescence characteristics of CdTe/CdS QDs were recorded at diverse excitation wavelengths from 270 to 320 nm. The optimal emission peaked at 565 nm under the excitation at 280 nm. The effect of pH on the fluorescence properties of CdTe/CdS QDs was studied by measuring the emission intensity of different pH solutions at 565 nm in the range of 2 to 13. As shown in Fig. 2B, in the wide pH range of 5.0 to 12.0, the fluorescence intensity of the CdTe/CdS QDs would not affected by the change of pH value. Also, the effect of ionic strength on CdTe/CdS QDs was evaluated in different NaCl solutions ranging from 0 to 500 mM: with the change in NaCl concentration, the strength did not change significantly (Fig. 2C), indicating that the influence of ionic strength on the detection of various concentrations can be neglected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and IO3 − integrated into CdTe/CdS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: For a sensor array, it is a challenge to discriminate different ions at various concentration levels. As can be seen from the Fig. 3A–C, the FL intensity of CdTe/CdS QDs decreases at 565 nm when various target ions were added in the corresponding buffer solution and show a good linear relationship. In order to verify that the system can efficiently distinguish between different targets, the emission intensity with different manner was used for clustering analysis of the different targets. Experiments have shown that the response from one ion at different concentrations forms obvious clustering around the center, therefore, the CdTe/CdS could be utilized to differentiate responding mechanisms with fingerprint mapping of fluorescence intensities to distinguish Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and IO3 − (Fig. 3D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-mode sensing Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and IO3 − integrated into CdTe/CdS QDs
Detection of Fe3+ in a PET manner
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the selectivity of the CdTe/CdS QDs nanosensor for Fe3+, the change of FL intensity with the presence of various ions was investigated. It observed that the CdTe/CdS QDs shows high selectivity to Fe3+, while other metal ions barely exerted influence (Fig. 4A). The mechanism of QDs fluorescence quenching induced by Fe3+ ions can be accredited to the specific interaction on the surface of CdTe/CdS QDs (formation of non-fluorescent complexes). As can be seen from the inset of Fig. 4B, it was negligible to the IFE effect due to without absorption of Fe3+ in the range from 200 to 500 nm. It was further confirmed by the competitive chelating agent EDTA was added to the CdTe/CdS QDs quenched by Fe3+, and the fluorescence intensity was restored (Fig. 4B). Due to the coordination interaction, excited-state electron of QDs will be transferred to the unfilled orbital of the Fe3+, resulting in fluorescence quenching. As shown in Fig. 3A, a good linear range was observed between the concentration of Fe3+ from 5 to 100 μM with regression equation as follow: (F0 − F) / F0 = 0.00425 + 0.0011c, and the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 4.1 μM (LOD = 3Sd/s). Therefore, the CdTe/CdS QDs offer the opportunity to develop as a fluorescence probe for sensing of Fe3+ in the sodium acetate-acetic acid buffer solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr2O7 2− in an IFE manner
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the specificity of CdTe/CdS QDs for Cr2O7 2−, the FL intensity of QDs at 565 nm was recorded for the presence of potentially interfering substances (200 μM), including Mg2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe3+, K+, MnO4 −, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Na+, Fe2+, Mn2+. As shown in Fig. 5A, with the exception of only Cr2O7 2−, which can significantly quench fluorescence, the effects of other substances on the FL intensity of QDs were negligible. More interestingly, no significant quenching of Fe3+ occurred in the PBS solution, which might be due to the formation of Fe3+ and PBS solution more stable the masking effect of the composite [41]. Accordingly, we found that the FL intensity of CdTe/CdS QDs-based sensors was quenched by Cr2O7 2− in a concentration dependence, described by the following equation: (F0 − F)/F0 = 0.0039 + 0.0155c (R2 = 0.994), and the LOD was estimated to be 9.7 μΜ (Fig. 3B). Fig. 5B further illustrated the quenching mechanism through the UV–vis spectrophotometer. The absorption spectrum of Cr2O7 2− exhibits absorption at 358 and 258 nm, and a considerable overlapped with the excitation spectrum of the CdTe/CdS QDs (Fig. 5C). Notably, the fluorescence decay curves showed that the fluorescence lifetime of CdTe/CdS QDs was almost unchanged in the presence of different concentration of Cr2O7 2−(fluorescence quenching), indicating that the fluorescence change of CdTe/CdS QDs is a photophysical process occurs at the ground state rather than ascribed to the excited state perturbation (Fig. S2) [42]. This phenomenon is the so-called IFE, which can remarkably enhance the sensitivity and selectivity compared to the other fluorescence quenching manner. In summary, this method proposed the potential advantages for determination of Cr2O7 2− in IFE manner.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of IO3 − in an OQ manner
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 3C, the peak intensity at 568 nm and the concentration of iodate in the range of 1.0–80.0 μM reveal a good linear relationship. The regression equation is (F0 − F) / F0 = 0.00732–0.0291c (R2 = 0.989, LOD = 0.9 μM). To investigate the specificity of the CdTe/CdS QDs for interfering ions, fluorescence was recorded for different anions on QDs (Fig. 6A). IO3 − exerted significant quenching effect on CdTe/CdS QDs under excitation at 280 nm, while other interfering anions slightly interfere. In addition, the coexistence of a mixture of IO3 − with common anions played little role on fluorescence quenching (Table S2). As illustrated in Fig. 6B, as the iodate concentration increases, the yellow-green fluorescence intensity decreases. On the other hand, with the increase of IO3 − concentration, a slight blue shift in the emission spectrum can be observed, indicating a decrease in the particle size of the CdTe/CdS QDs. To verify the detection mechanism might the QDs were oxidized by iodate, a series of experiments were performed (Fig. 6C). Adding IO3 − (100 mM) aqueous solution and 200 μL of CdTe/CdS QDs solution to 1 mL of 0.5% starch solution, no color change was observed even after a few days. Notably, the color immediately turned blue when a few drops of phosphoric acid were added to the mixture. This fact was owing to that the reaction of IO3 − and CdTe/CdS QDs producing iodine anions, and the reaction between iodine and iodate anion was quite inconspicuous when iodine ion is strongly adsorbed on the surface of quantum dots. However, with the phosphoric acid the reaction between the iodide and excess IO3 − was accelerated, resulting in the production of I2. As a contrast, under the same experimental conditions, no color change was observed in the iodate-MPA-starch solution mixture (0.05% MPA used), so the possible reaction between IO3 − and MPA can be excluded. Therefore, the CdTe/CdS QDs can be offered potentiality as a probe for the detection of IO3 − by OQ mechanism, which was rarely reported so far.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and IO3 − in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the high efficiency, reliability and stability of CdTe/CdS QDs, it is useful to detect Fe3+, Cr2O7 2−, and IO3 − in real samples. Herein, we investigated according to the standard addition method and tested Xinkai lake water, Mati lake water, and tap water as the real samples. As shown in Table 1 , standard addition experiment indicated that the recoveries by proposed method for detection of Fe3+ were in the range of 102.3%–105.4%, Cr2O7 2− in the range of 93.6%–108.5%, and IO3 − in the range of 92.8%–98.5%. According to previous reports [43], the iodide was oxidized with bromine water. Briefly, 10 mL of table salt sample (0.1 g mL−1), 2 mL of a saturated bromine aqueous solution, and a small amount of HCl solution (pH = 3) were placed in this order. Then after stirring and boiling about 10 min, the mixture became colorless to for all bromine was expelled. Therefore, iodide in the sample can be quantitated into iodate. This method can be used to detect the total amount of iodate and iodine in table salt, and the corresponding results were listed in Table 2 . In summary, the results indicated that the method can be used to intuitively distinguish different kinds of ions and analyze the real samples.

In conclusion, we have fabricated the CdTe/CdS QDs via a one-pot solvothermal method for the detection of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2− and IO3 − by three varying types of modes including PET, IFE and OQ. The CdTe/CdS QDs can be used for selective and effective detection of Fe3+, Cr2O7 2−, and IO3 − with a detection limit of 4.1, 9.7 and 0.9 μM, respectively. Moreover, the as-prepared CdTe/CdS QDs-based platform was also successfully and efficiently applied to detect in real samples. Besides, it was successfully detected the total amount of iodate and iodine in salt samples. Therefore, the proposed strategy was simple, intuitive, highly sensitive and selective, and it is possible to be further provided a new idea for the development of multi-mode fluorescence sensors.
Wei Wei: Resources, Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft. Jun Fan: Investigation. Yan Xia: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Xuebo Yin: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874073) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118630.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A multidimensional sensing device for the discrimination of proteins based on manganese-doped ZnS quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Wu, L.-N. Miao, H.-F. Wang, X.-G. Shao, X.-P. Yan, A Multidimensional Sensing Device for the Discrimination of Proteins Based on Manganese-Doped ZnS Quantum Dots, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed., 50 (2011) 8118-8121.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots originated from methyl red with molecular state and surface state controlled emissions for sensing and imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Pan, Y. Zhang, X. Sun, W. Pan, G. Yu, Q. Zhao, J. Wang, Carbon dots originated from methyl red with molecular state and surface state controlled emissions for sensing and imaging, J. Lumin., 204 (2018) 303-311.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multidimensional colorimetric sensor array for discrimination of proteins

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Mao, Y. Lu, N. Chang, J. Yang, S. Zhang, Y. Liu, Multidimensional colorimetric sensor array for discrimination of proteins, Biosens. Bioelectron., 86 (2016) 56-61.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Light-induced self-assembly of bi-color CdTe quantum dots allows the discrimination of multiple proteins

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Ma, H. Liu, G. Wu, Z. Liu, P. Wu, L. Li, Light-induced self-assembly of bi-color CdTe quantum dots allows the discrimination of multiple proteins, J. Mater. Chem. B, 5 (2017) 5745-5752.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Excitation dependent emission combined with different quenching manners supports carbon dots to achieve multi-mode sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Chun, W. Liu, X. Sun, W. Pan, G. Yu, J. Wang, Excitation dependent emission combined with different quenching manners supports carbon dots to achieve multi-mode sensing, Sens. Actuators, B, 263 (2018) 1-9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design strategies for lab-on-a-molecule probes and orthogonal sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Chen, Q. Shu, M. Schmittel, Design strategies for lab-on-a-molecule probes and orthogonal sensing, Chem. Soc. Rev., 44 (2015) 136-160.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lab-on-MOFs: color-coded multitarget fluorescence detection with white-light emitting metal-organic frameworks under single wavelength excitation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.M. Wang, Z.R. Yang, L. Xiao, X.B. Yin, Lab-on-MOFs: Color-Coded Multitarget Fluorescence Detection with White-Light Emitting Metal-Organic Frameworks under Single Wavelength Excitation, Anal. Chem., 90 (2018) 5758-5763.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Plasma-assisted quadruple-channel optosensing of proteins and cells with Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Li, P. Wu, X. Hou, Plasma-assisted quadruple-channel optosensing of proteins and cells with Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots, Nanoscale, 8 (2016) 4291-4298.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.G. Reshma, P.V. Mohanan, Quantum dots: Applications and safety consequences, J. Lumin., 205 (2019) 287-298.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Ahumada-Lazo, S.M. Fairclough, S.J.O. Hardman, P.N. Taylor, M. Green, S.J. Haigh, R. Saran, R.J. Curry, D.J. Binks, Confinement Effects and Charge Dynamics in Zn3N2 Colloidal Quantum Dots: Implications for QD-LED Displays, ACS Appl. Nano Mater, 2 (2019) 7214-7219.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Gu, L. Zou, Z. Fang, W. Zhu, X. Zhong, One-pot synthesis of highly luminescent CdTe/CdS core/shell nanocrystals in aqueous phase, Nanotechnology, 19 (2008) 135604.

T. Maxwell, M.G. Nogueira Campos, S. Smith, M. Doomra, Z. Thwin, S. Santra, Chapter 15 - Quantum Dots, in: E.J. Chung, L. Leon, C. Rinaldi (Eds.) Nanoparticles for Biomedical Applications, Elsevier, 2020, pp. 243-265.

J. Am. Assoc. Lab. Anim. Sci.

M.B. Zimmermann, Iodine Deficiency, Endocr. Rev., 30 (2009) 376-408.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Li, W. Liu, X. Sun, W. Pan, J. Wang, Multi sensing functions integrated into one carbon-dot based platform via different types of mechanisms, Sens. Actuators, B, 252 (2017) 544-553.
